content:
# Matthew Chapter 6 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_006_page_001.jpg, MAT_006_page_002.jpg, MAT_006_page_003.jpg

### Giving to the Needy

1. "Be careful not to perform your acts of righteousness in front of others just to be noticed. If you do, you will have no reward from your Father in heaven.

2. So when you give to the poor, don't announce it with fanfare the way the hypocrites do in the synagogues and streets, to win people's admiration. I tell you the truth: they have already received their full reward.

3. But when you give to the poor, don't even let your left hand know what your right hand is doing.

4. Give your gifts in secret, and your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.

### Prayer

5. And when you pray, don't be like the hypocrites who love to pray standing in the synagogues and on the street corners where everyone can see them. I tell you the truth: they have already received their full reward.

6. But when you pray, go into your room, close the door, and pray to your Father in that secret place. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.

7. When you pray, don't babble on and on like the pagans, who think God will hear them because of their many words.

8. Don't be like them. Your Father already knows what you need before you ask him.

9. This is how you should pray: 'Our Father in heaven, may your name be honored as holy.

10. May your kingdom come. May your will be carried out on earth as it is in heaven.

11. Give us today the bread we need for this day.

12. Forgive us what we owe, as we have forgiven those who owe us.

13. And do not lead us into a time of testing, but rescue us from the evil one.'

14. For if you forgive others when they wrong you, your heavenly Father will also forgive you.

15. But if you refuse to forgive others, your Father will not forgive your wrongs either.

### Fasting

16. When you fast, don't put on a miserable face like the hypocrites do. They make themselves look pale and disheveled so everyone will know they're fasting. I tell you the truth: they have already received their full reward.

17. But when you fast, wash your face and groom yourself as usual,

18. so that no one will know you're fasting — except your Father, who sees what is done in secret. And your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you.

### Treasures in Heaven

19. Don't stockpile wealth here on earth, where moths and corrosion eat it away and thieves break in and steal it.

20. Instead, store up your wealth in heaven, where no moth or corrosion can destroy it and no thief can break in and steal it.

21. For wherever your treasure is, that's where your heart will be too.

22. The eye is the body's lamp. If your eye is generous, your whole body is flooded with light.

23. But if your eye is stingy, your whole body is plunged into darkness. And if the light inside you is actually darkness — what total darkness that is!

24. No one can serve two masters. You will end up hating one and loving the other, or devoted to one and contemptuous of the other. You cannot serve both God and Money.

### Do Not Worry

25. That's why I tell you: stop worrying about your life — what you'll eat, what you'll drink, what you'll wear. Isn't life more than food? Isn't the body more than clothing?

26. Look at the birds. They don't plant or harvest or store food in barns, yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Aren't you worth far more than they are?

27. Can any of you add a single hour to your life by worrying?

28. And why worry about clothing? Look at how the wildflowers grow. They don't work or weave fabric.

29. Yet I tell you that not even Solomon at the height of his splendor was dressed as beautifully as one of these.

30. If God clothes the wild grass — here today, fuel for the fire tomorrow — won't he much more clothe you? How little faith you have!

31. So don't worry, saying, 'What will we eat?' or 'What will we drink?' or 'What will we wear?'

32. The pagans run after all these things. But your heavenly Father knows you need every one of them.

33. Instead, make the kingdom of God and his righteousness your first pursuit, and all these things will be given to you as well.

34. So don't worry about tomorrow. Tomorrow will have worries of its own. Each day has enough trouble as it is."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "announce it with fanfare":** The Greek μὴ σαλπίσῃς literally means "do not trumpet." Whether Jesus meant an actual trumpet or a metaphor for ostentatious display is debated, but the force is clear: don't turn charity into a performance.

**Verse 9 — "may your name be honored as holy":** The Greek ἁγιασθήτω τὸ ὄνομά σου means "let your name be sanctified/made holy." But God's name doesn't need to be *made* holy — it already is. The prayer asks that God's name be *treated as* holy, *recognized as* holy, *honored as* holy in the world. This is not a request to change God but to change the world's relationship to God.

**Verse 11 — "the bread we need for this day":** The mysterious word ἐπιούσιον (found nowhere else in Greek literature) likely means something like "necessary for existence" or "for the coming day." "The bread we need for this day" captures the sense of daily dependence without pretending to resolve the linguistic puzzle.

**Verse 13 — no doxology:** The familiar ending "For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, forever. Amen" is NOT in the Codex Sinaiticus. It was added later from liturgical usage. The prayer in its earliest form ends with the raw plea: *rescue us from the evil one*. The prayer ends not with triumph but with dependence.

**Verses 22-23 — "generous/stingy" for ἁπλοῦς/πονηρός:** In Jewish culture, a "good eye" (עַיִן טוֹבָה) meant generosity, and an "evil eye" (עַיִן רָעָה) meant stinginess or envy. Jesus is not talking about eyesight — he's talking about how you see other people and their needs. A generous outlook fills your whole life with light; a grasping, envious outlook darkens everything.

**Verse 24 — "Money" with capital M:** The Greek μαμωνᾶ (from Aramaic) personifies wealth as a rival deity. Capitalizing "Money" captures this personification — Jesus is not merely warning about being materialistic. He is saying that wealth demands worship, and you must choose your god.

**Verse 28 — "wildflowers" for κρίνα:** The Greek κρίνον is traditionally rendered "lilies" but likely refers to various wildflowers of the Galilean hillsides — anemones, poppies, and others. "Wildflowers" captures the natural, unforced beauty Jesus is pointing to.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
